task.domains: (topic task) (topic hi-d) (topic ui-d) (topic sw-d) (topic indexing-d) (topic ut-d) a(base ishcondition) a(base varid) a(base varref) a(base cid) a(base partslist) (topic oce-d) (topic sdlVariables-c) (topic oceCommonElements-c) (topic oceMetaDecl-c) (topic oceTopic-c) (topic oceTopic-c task oceTask-c) (topic oceTopic-c hi-d oceHighlight-c) (topic oceTopic-c ui-d oceUi-c) (topic oceTopic-c sw-d oceSoftware-c)
task.title: 07 The function of the Maintenance and Tissue Wipe Unit

abstract.p: The maintenance unit is located on the right hand side of the printer and consists of:

li.p: Clean tray unit: this unit holds the disposable Tissue wipe cassette containing the tissue and realizes correct cassette positioning and movements.
Purge tray unit: this unit holds the heated funnel to collect purge liquids and ink leakages from the printheads and transport them to the liquid waste unit.
Air Pressure Supply Unit (APSU)
Pressure Manifolds. Required to enable choosing which printheads to purge.

p.b: The maintenance unit has the following main functions:

abstract.p: The basis of printhead maintenance tissue wiper is:

li.p: To keep the M10 nozzle plate and Front Finish Plate [FFP] free from ink residues.
To keep CYMKW ink in the print head in good condition.
To keep the nozzle plate in good condition at the start of a print.

abstract.p: Collection of residual ink from the purge and spitting actions in a maintenance tray.
Create an under or over-pressure for the CYMKW print heads.

section.p: Within the tissue wipe concept, different process flows have been defined to ensure a clean and dry nozzle plate.
These process flows are initiated by maintenance strategies, specifically designed for the Colorado M-series configuration with inks.
Inline print quality measurements (e.g. PAINt) are used as input parameters within maintenance strategies.

section.title: The functional steps of the tissue wiper
section.p: The tissue wiper steps are:

li.p: This action flushes the nozzles.
During a purge ink is dripping out of the nozzles into the purge tray unit.
For this purpose the purge tray unit is positioned below the printheads.
From the purge tray unit the ink flows into the waste ink container which collects all waste ink.
After a purge action the nozzle plate is wiped with a tissue.
This results in a dry and clean nozzle plate.
The cassette moves up to the nozzle plate and the tissue is transported to refresh the tissue continuously.
Ink is absorbed by the tissue and is disposed via the tissue.
During a wipe the tissue is in contact with the nozzle plate with a pre-defined pressure.
Spit (during printing modus and stand-by modus):
This action is the jetting of ink and it is performed to keep the nozzle plate dry and keep nozzles in right condition.
This can be performed in standby, and during printing.
Spitting is performed in the purge tray unit.
In standby mode the print heads are positioned above the purge tray unit.
Ink leakages can be collected here without contaminating the engine.

section.p: Besides automatic maintenance also manual maintenance procedures have been developed.
These procedures are required periodically and in case of a calamity.

The periodic manual maintenance is triggered via a counter and includes the cleaning of the matrix plate and surroundings, and the maintenance unit surroundings by using a swab stick, towels and methyl benzoate.
This is a Key Operator Maintenance action, it is not allowed to touch the nozzle plate.
In case a printhead recovery is needed a soaking action can be performed by service.
Dabbing is no longer part of the manual maintenance.

li.p: The cassette is disposable and can be replaced by the customer when the tissue is finished.
Two printheads are cleaned per action (CK/YM/W [2 Print heads per colour] where the tissue absorbs the ink.
Number of wipes per day: 5 x 3 = 15; 1 cassette/month on average usage (15K sqm/yr); total time = 5 x 1.5 minute = 7.5 minutes per day.

section.p: Spitting volumes need to be collected. In the past this was done on paper, however, the large amounts of spitting volumes will be visible on paper.
To overcome this problem the following three scenario's have been analyzed:

li.p: Spit on paper via spitbar
Spit in tissue
Spit in purge tray unit (PTU)

section.p: The new purge tray unit (funnel) has been designed to collect purged, spitted and leaked ink and transport these volumes to the liquid waste unit.
The purge tray unit (funnel) is heated in order to let the ink flow.

li.p: Temperature: Tmin: 50 ºC, Ttarget: 70 ºC, Tmax: 85 ºC
Heating must occur on regular basis to prevent waste build up in the purge tray unit
Heating is timer based to switch off after reaching the setpoint time.

section.title: Maintenance tray unit
section.p: A sensor at the rear of the unit detects the maximum ink level in the maintenance tray.
The volume of the maintenance tray is 1.2L.
Almost full level warning @82% = 656 ml.
Total volume in Maintenance Tray @100% = 800 ml.
In the pictures you see examples of a rather empty maintenance tray and an almost full maintenance tray.
The pressure manifold is located at the printhead carriage.
A pressure manifold is required to enable choosing which heads to purge.
The pressure manifold consists of an air supply, a valve and an air outlet.
The pressure manifold is designed for pressure from -15 mbar to 250 mbar.
The main function of the Air Pressure Supply Unit is:

li.p: Maintain an under pressure to prevent ink-leakage of the printheads.
Provide pressure pulses for purging of the printhead.
A flow restriction.
PBA with the air pressure sensor.
A buffer vessel.

section.p: In general the operation of the unit proceeds in 4 steps.

li.p: At startup, calaibrate the pressure sensor by storing the sensor reading at ambient pressure
Under pressure used in different states
During printing and ready to print state the pressure is kept on -10 mbar.
During all other states the under-pressure at the printheads must be between -20 and -6 mbar
In this state the air flow from the APSU to the printheads is almost zero, so the pressure measured at the APSU is representative for the pressure at the printheads.
Provide a (short) over-pressure pulse of 160 or 240 mbar to purge the printheads during cleaning.
Flush out a small amount of ink from the nozzles.
When the system is powered down, maintain sufficient under-pressure on the printheads for specified time.
